about: Per-repo assessment of what each surveyed project's OWN published output shows about provider-routing corruption. Evidence-side counterpart to the code audit in survey.json: this asks what a skeptical READER could see, using the fingerprint catalogue in findings/fingerprints.json.

provenance.method: One agent per repo hunted F1-F17 fingerprints in the project's released artifacts (papers, HF datasets, committed run logs, leaderboards) — opening files, not inferring from directory listings. Every repo that returned a positive verdict (fingerprints_found or checked_clean) was then re-checked by an adversarial verifier that re-downloaded the cited evidence and downgraded overreach. Cell prose was finally rewritten into the dataset's impersonal register without adding claims.

verdicts.fingerprints_found: Something in the project's own published output is visibly off in a way provider routing or quantization would explain.
verdicts.checked_clean: They released enough for a reader to look, and the checks that apply come back clean.
verdicts.inconclusive: Raw outputs exist but the check does not settle it either way.
verdicts.nothing_checkable_released: No transcripts, per-sample data, or provider metadata were published, so no reader could ever check. The expected default.

provenance.caveat: A `checked_clean` verdict is scoped to the fingerprints listed, on the data actually released. It is not a clean bill of health for the serving stack — most releases carry no provider metadata at all, so F2 usually stays open even where the text looks fine. Conversely `fingerprints_found` means an observation consistent with bad serving survived adversarial review; it does not by itself prove any reported number is wrong.

AI Diplomacy.text: This project published far more than bare aggregate numbers, but stopped well short of a systematic raw-transcript release, so the fingerprint checks that matter most (F2 provider IDs, F3 truncation rates, F5 reasoning-shape uniformity, F11 uneven parse failures) cannot be run at scale. The public raw text is limited to a few paper case-study quotes and one committed markdown report (experiments/test_lie_fix.md), both specific to the OpenRouter-routed open-weight powers (Llama-4-Maverick, Gemini-2.5-Flash-preview via OpenRouter); the sample was directly opened, not inferred from a directory listing, and reads as fully coherent English/JSON with no token soup (F1), no special-token or chat-template litter, and no U+FFFD (F4). That is a genuine, if thin, negative check against those two fingerprints. But the excerpts are hand-picked "interesting" moments embedded in a betrayal-detection report and a persuasion case study, not a representative dump, so cherry-picking cannot be ruled out, and the underlying llm_responses.csv / lmvsgame.json files (the artifacts that would carry any provider/generation-id or finish_reason field) are gitignored and never released. The paper's acknowledgments confirm OpenRouter usage ("We thank Cohere, OpenAI, and OpenRouter for generously providing API credits"), but no provider, quantization, or snapshot pin is disclosed anywhere for the open-weight models in Table 2 (F16, weak alone). Net: some real output text exists and looks clean where readable, but the release is too partial to settle whether serving corrupted the reported scores.
AI Diplomacy.what_is_published: Aggregate numbers only, plus a handful of cherry-picked raw-text excerpts (no systematic transcript release). Concretely: (1) the NeurIPS-workshop paper arXiv:2508.07485 ("Democratizing Diplomacy") contains bar charts/tables of game scores, invalid-order rates, betrayal rates, and Chatbot-Arena correlations (Table 2, Figs 3-16) for OpenRouter-routed open-weight models (Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B-A22B, qwq-32b, Mistral-Small-3.2, gemma-3-12b, c4ai-command-a, Kimi-K2, Devstral-Small) alongside two narrative "case studies" that quote a handful of real negotiation messages and diary excerpts (Fig. 25-26, e.g. Kimi-K2's ultimatum to Devstral-Small, quoted diary line "A TUS is positioned to threaten Italy..."). (2) The GitHub repo github.com/GoodStartLabs/AI_Diplomacy has `results/`, `logs/`, `game.json` etc. explicitly in `.gitignore` -- no raw `llm_responses.csv` / `lmvsgame.json` from any real game is committed. The one committed game-analysis report, experiments/test_lie_fix.md, quotes several real diary/message fragments from one specific game including from the OpenRouter-routed powers AUSTRIA (openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick) and GERMANY (openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20), one of which is explicitly labeled "Raw diary: ```json {...}" i.e. unparsed raw model output. (3) visualization_results/ contains only aggregate PNGs/CSvs (analysis_metadata.json: "total_games": 4004, 61 unique models) -- no per-response text. (4) The synthetic ai_animation/public/test-game.json used for UI testing contains only literal "PLACEHOLDER_MESSAGE_N" strings, not real data. No HuggingFace dataset, Zenodo artifact, or GitHub Release with data assets exists for this repo/org.

AI Induced Psychosis: A shallow investig.text: Fingerprint checks come back mostly clean. None of the published transcripts or JSON dumps contain special-token litter, U+FFFD, or provider/generation-id keys (F4 negative), and F2 is unobtainable: the published `_redteam.json` files are simplified message dumps with `metadata: null` and no provider/gen-id field at all, so this project cannot be checked for F2 either way from what it released; the lone error_logs file does show OpenRouter's raw error passthrough naming `provider_name: xAI` for one grok-4 403, a single data point, not proof of anything. Reasoning blocks for deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss-20b (e.g. `full_transcripts/Frank_openrouter-openai-gpt-oss-20b_..._target.md`) read as coherent, on-topic chain-of-thought rather than token soup (F1 negative), and both models show a perfectly uniform 1 reasoning-block-per-assistant-turn ratio across all 8 characters (checked_clean for those two). The grader-failure rate (-1/missing `delusion_confirmation_rating` in `result_grades/redteaming_results_20250819_100654.csv`) is low (0.9-4.6%) and is not concentrated on OpenRouter-served models versus the direct OpenAI/Anthropic arms — a clean F11 read. The one open item is gemini-2.5-pro, where reasoning-block presence varies transcript-to-transcript (3/12 to 12/12 assistant turns) with no consistent turn-position pattern (e.g. `Colin_openrouter-google-gemini-2.5-pro_..._target.md` reasons on turns 1, 2, and 10 only; `Nathan_...` reasons on turns 1-6 then never again), matching F5's shape. Whether this reflects a serving artifact or Google's own documented behavior that Gemini thought-summaries are not guaranteed on every turn even via the first-party API cannot be settled from what is published, since no non-OpenRouter comparison transcript exists to check against — hence inconclusive rather than a confirmed fingerprint.
AI Induced Psychosis: A shallow investig.what_is_published: The repo (github.com/tim-hua-01/ai-psychosis) publishes real per-turn data, not just aggregates: `full_transcripts/` contains ~120 markdown transcripts (one per character x model) rendering user/assistant turns and, where returned, a separate "Assistant Reasoning" block, plus a handful of raw `_redteam.json` message dumps (inspect_ai ChatMessage-shaped: id/content/source/metadata/internal/role); `result_grades/` has CSV grader outputs per turn (1032 rows in the main run) with per-model rating columns including a -1 sentinel for ungraded/failed turns; `error_logs/` has one full JSON error dump including the raw OpenRouter error body. I opened ~40 of these files directly (via raw.githubusercontent.com), not just directory listings.

ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundati.text: This project publishes the OpenRouter-using harness code but no raw output from any actual benchmarked model. github.com/arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking's results/ and data/sample/submissions/ directories were opened directly, not inferred from a directory listing: the only content in them is a random-baseline aggregate-metrics file and one fabricated example submission whose message field literally reads "ARC task prompt omitted for brevity" — useful only to show the harness's JSON schema (which does include a per-attempt "provider" and "model" key, and a "choices" transcript field), not as evidence about real runs. The paper PDF (arXiv 2505.11831) was fetched and read in full and contains no appendix of chain-of-thought transcripts and no mention of OpenRouter, provider routing, or quantization anywhere in the text. arcprize.org/leaderboard, fetched directly, shows only aggregate score/cost-per-task numbers, no raw completions, no per-provider breakdown, and no named quantization/precision. Users who want real per-model outputs must run the harness themselves against their own API keys; the org has not committed or hosted that data anywhere findable — no HF dataset, no Zenodo record, no results release on GitHub. This is the expected default case: the harness is real and does route certain configs through OpenRouter (a code-level risk already established), but nothing in what ARC Prize actually published lets a reader inspect a single real transcript, so no fingerprint check (F1–F9) is possible.
ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundati.what_is_published: Only the benchmarking harness code itself (github.com/arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking) plus two synthetic/placeholder examples used to test the harness: results/random-baseline-sample/results.json (aggregate metrics only — score/cost/duration per task, no completions, from a random-guessing baseline, not a real LLM) and data/sample/submissions/example_model/66e6c45b.json (a hand-authored fake submission with model:"sample-model", provider:"sample", and message content literally reading "ARC task prompt omitted for brevity" / "Predicted grid output"). No directory of real per-model generations, no HuggingFace/Zenodo dataset, and no appendix transcripts in the paper (arXiv 2505.11831) were found. The public leaderboard (arcprize.org/leaderboard) shows only aggregate score-vs-cost numbers per model, no raw outputs or per-provider breakdown.
ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundati.evidence: Repo contents checked via GitHub API: `results/` -> only `random-baseline-sample/results.json` (844 bytes, aggregate metrics, no message content) at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking/main/results/random-baseline-sample/results.json. `data/sample/submissions/example_model/66e6c45b.json` at https://raw.githubusercontent.com/arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking/main/data/sample/submissions/example_model/66e6c45b.json contains model:"sample-model", provider:"sample", and content:"ARC task prompt omitted for brevity" / "Predicted grid output" — explicitly a fabricated schema example, not a real run. README.md (fetched raw) instructs users to run models themselves with their own keys and save to `submissions/<config>/`; no leaderboard/HF/Zenodo link for raw outputs is given. WebFetch of arxiv.org/pdf/2505.11831 found no OpenRouter/provider/quantization mentions and no transcript appendix in the 13-page paper. WebFetch of arcprize.org/leaderboard confirmed aggregate-only display, no provider or precision info. Note: an unrelated HuggingFace search result ("schema-harness/arc-agi-3-schema-traces", mentioning fictional models "GPT-5.6 Sol" and "Claude Fable 5") surfaced during search but is from a different, unaffiliated HF author and is not connected to arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking or this paper — disregarded as noise, not evidence.

AgentLab (ServiceNow Research).text: tmlr_traces_part_aa is genuinely gzip-compressed (magic bytes 1f8b) and is a raw byte-split of a single continuous gzip stream that decompresses correctly even from a truncated prefix. In .../multi-chart-value-retrieval_102/, experiment.log records a POST to https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions returning "HTTP/1.1 200 OK", followed by chain-of-thought text referencing "Canon" and "Configuration Item by Manufacturer" and the action click('a311'); the accompanying summary_info.json shows n_steps=3, terminated=true, truncated=false, max_output_tokens=150, cum_cost=0.02317692. The stuck-loop episode at .../workload-balancing-small-l2_102/experiment.log, roughly 3.5GB into the stream, contains exactly 42 occurrences of send_msg_to_user, with coherent, grammatical, non-degenerate repeated text — a genuine stuck agentic loop rather than a serving artifact. Both logs decode as UTF-8 with zero replacement characters and zero `<|...|>` token-leakage markers. A regex string-extraction pass over the raw pickle bytes of step_0.pkl.gz, searching for provider/gen-id/response_id/chatcmpl, returns zero hits: this data does not surface provider or generation-ID metadata without unpickling through the agentlab package, so that dimension remains unchecked rather than shown clean. The coverage behind these findings is limited — 9 episodes and one model (llama-3.1-405b-instruct) out of six evaluated, drawn from roughly 3.5GB of the 207GB corpus (476 experiment.log files turn up in that slice alone) — a spot-check, not exhaustive coverage.
AgentLab (ServiceNow Research).what_is_published: A 207GB raw-trace dataset from the BrowserGym/AgentLab TMLR paper (arXiv:2412.05467) at https://huggingface.co/datasets/agentlabtraces/agentlabtraces/tree/main, split into 5 gzip-tar volumes (tmlr_traces_part_aa..ae). I independently confirmed this is the project's own official release, not a third-party mirror: ServiceNow/AgentLab's own README.md (line 340) links this exact HF URL as "Traces from 'The BrowserGym Ecosystem for Web Agent Research' paper," and the repo's reproducibility_journal.csv attributes benchmark runs to the "agentlabtraces" identity. Each episode contains exp_args.pkl, a plaintext experiment.log with full per-step CoT text and actions, per-step step_N.pkl.gz observations, screenshots, and summary_info.json with token/cost/truncation stats.
AgentLab (ServiceNow Research).evidence: Confirmed live: HF dataset tree (5 parts, 207GB total, tmlr_traces_part_aa = 42,949,672,960 bytes via HTTP header x-linked-size). ServiceNow/AgentLab README.md:340 links this exact dataset URL as the paper's trace release; reproducibility_journal.csv references "agentlabtraces" runs. Extracted and byte-matched: tmlr_exps/2024-10-25_17-34-49_.../multi-chart-value-retrieval_102/experiment.log line 15 = "2024-10-25 17:35:13,880 - 1321087 - httpx - INFO - HTTP Request: POST https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \"HTTP/1.1 200 OK\"", CoT text about "Canon"/"Configuration Item by Manufacturer" and click('a311') -- exact match. summary_info.json for same episode: n_steps=3, terminated=true, truncated=false, stats.max_output_tokens=150, stats.cum_cost=0.02317692 -- exact match. workload-balancing-small-l2_102/experiment.log: grep -c "send_msg_to_user" = 42, exact match; text is coherent English, not gibberish. step_0.pkl.gz for the Canon episode: Python regex string-extraction (22,257 printable ASCII runs) for provider|gen-|openrouter|response_id|chatcmpl returned 0 hits, confirming F2 was correctly left unchecked rather than falsely claimed clean.

Aider (AI pair-programming CLI + polyglo.text: Aider's blog post at aider.chat/2025/05/08/qwen3.html reports four diff-format benchmark rows for Qwen3-235B-A22B: 61.3% on VLLM with bfloat16 (no-think), 59.6% via the Alibaba API (no-think), 54.7% on OpenRouter pinned to TogetherAI via `provider: order: ["Together"]` with recommended sampling params and no-think (cost $0.64, matching the page's `data-cost="0.6399"` attribute), and 49.8% on OpenRouter with no provider pin ("all providers") and default settings (cost $1.80, matching `data-max-cost="1.8037"`). That unpinned 49.8% row was obtained by "simply running aider... without any model specific settings," which "should have enabled thinking" — so it differs from the three no-think rows by both provider-pinning and reasoning-mode/sampling-params at once, and cannot isolate a provider effect on its own. Restricting the comparison to the three no-think rows — VLLM-bf16 61.3%, Alibaba-API 59.6%, OpenRouter-pinned-to-Together 54.7% — leaves a 6.6-point gap across serving backends, consistent with Aider's own observation that proprietary models are served by their creators or trusted providers with stable inference settings while API providers "can use very different inference settings, quantizations, etc." Separately, the shipped polyglot_leaderboard.yml entry for Qwen3-32B runs the command `aider --model openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b` with no provider order specified, scoring pass_rate_2 40.0, while the file's Qwen3-235B-A22B row (`aider --model openai/qwen3-235b-a22b`, pass_rate_2 59.6) corresponds to the pinned Alibaba-endpoint condition rather than an unpinned OpenRouter run.

A GitHub code search across Aider-AI org for "aider.chat.history.md" returns only doc mentions of the filename, never a committed instance. The community mirror aj47/techfren-leaderboard's full file tree contains only src/data/models.yaml (aggregate) plus a Next.js frontend — no transcripts.

AppWorld.text: The flagged pathway (`experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/{meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter,deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter}/*.jsonnet`, which set `"name": "openrouter/..."` via litellm with no provider pin) exists as committed source, but produced zero published output to inspect. The leaderboard repo's actual `_leaderboard.json` and its `experiments/outputs/` directory listing (17 experiments x 2 splits, all fetched via the GitHub API), plus every submission PR (#1-#14), were checked directly: every real, scored, bundle-backed submission uses OpenAI-direct, Together, or vendor-direct (Alibaba, Moonshot, Z.ai) endpoints, not OpenRouter. AppWorld does have a genuine raw-trajectory release mechanism (encrypted `.bundle` files, `appworld unpack` to decrypt) that would let a skeptical reader check provider/token-level fingerprints for a real submission, but no one has yet submitted, and the project has not published, any result that ran through the OpenRouter-routed configs. Nothing is checkable for this specific pathway because no output tied to it has been released.
AppWorld.what_is_published: The ACL'24 paper (arXiv 2407.18901, July 2024) predates OpenRouter use entirely and contains no OpenRouter mention or relevant transcripts. The companion github.com/StonyBrookNLP/appworld-leaderboard repo does publish raw per-task agent trajectories for every accepted leaderboard submission, as encrypted `experiments/outputs/{experiment}/leaderboard.bundle` files (`appworld unpack {experiment}` decrypts them; README: "We track experiment outputs in encrypted .bundle files to reduce the risk of it becoming part of the training corpora of LLMs") plus a machine-readable `_leaderboard.json` of scores. I fetched `_leaderboard.json` (18 entries) and listed all 34 dirs under `experiments/outputs/` (17 experiments x 2 splits), and none of them is a Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct or DeepSeek-R1-0528 OpenRouter run — every published entry is GPT-4o/GPT-4-Turbo/GPT-4.1 (OpenAI direct), LLaMA3-70B-chat-hf and DeepSeekCoder-33B (via together, not OpenRouter), Qwen2.5-32B (LOOP), or Qwen3-14B (AgentRL). I also listed all leaderboard-repo PRs (#1-#14, open and closed) submitting new results; none references an OpenRouter-routed model.

CAMEL (Communicative Agents for \"Mind\".text: The CAMEL paper, submitted March 31, 2023, makes no mention of OpenRouter anywhere in the arXiv abstract page or text — directly fetched and confirmed, not inferred — and OpenRouter itself did not yet exist at that time. The project's own published raw-output artifacts, the HuggingFace datasets camel-ai/math, ai_society, code, physics, chemistry, and biology, were generated by direct OpenAI API calls: the camel-ai/math dataset card states plainly "obtained using GPT-4," and independent summaries of the AI Society dataset confirm it was "synthetically generated by gpt-3.5-turbo." The one body of raw generations this project actually released never touched OpenRouter at all. In the current camel-ai/camel repo, OpenRouter appears only as one pluggable backend among dozens (`ModelPlatformType.OPENROUTER` → `OpenRouterModel`/`OpenRouterConfig`), demonstrated by a single toy example (`examples/models/openrouter_llama4_example.py`, using the free-tier Llama-4-Maverick with a "say hi" prompt) — library scaffolding for downstream users' own experiments, not a CAMEL research result. The repo's `camel/benchmarks/` directory (gaia.py, apibank.py, apibench.py, browsecomp.py, nexus.py, ragbench.py) and its `data/` directory (only role/task seed prompts, no generation logs) were directly opened, not inferred from a directory listing, and contain no committed run outputs, leaderboard rows, or transcripts produced via OpenRouter. Since no raw generation, transcript, or per-sample file that went through OpenRouter has ever been published by this project, nothing in CAMEL's own output bears on serving-corruption fingerprints — the code-level "at risk" finding concerns a backend option offered to third-party users of the library, not a path any CAMEL-published result traveled.
CAMEL (Communicative Agents for \"Mind\".what_is_published: The CAMEL paper (arXiv:2303.17760, NeurIPS 2023) and its released Hugging Face datasets (camel-ai/ai_society, camel-ai/code, camel-ai/math, camel-ai/physics, camel-ai/chemistry, camel-ai/biology — role-play conversations and problem/solution pairs). No provider/generation-id metadata, no per-response backend field, and no raw-transcript appendix tied to OpenRouter usage exist anywhere in these releases.

EQ-Bench Creative Writing Bench + Judgem.text: The token `</think>` appears exactly twice in creative_bench_runs.json and once in elo_results.json, and every occurrence traces to the same run, `run1__openrouter_hunter-alpha` (test_model='openrouter/hunter-alpha', judge_model='claude-sonnet-4-20250514'). At creative_tasks['2']['28'], the closing tag sits at character offset 6269 with generation_failed=False and a judge Overall Impression score of 16; the judge's raw_judge_text discusses "the piece" and "the ending" as one coherent story with no acknowledgment that the response contains a duplicated section, and the quote-mark style flips across that duplication boundary (45 curly quotes and 0 straight quotes in the first half, 0 curly and 45 straight in the second). At creative_tasks['2']['9'], leaked planning prose is immediately followed by ```html, again with generation_failed=False, and the identical text reappears verbatim in elo_results.json under 'openrouter/hunter-alpha' -> iterations['2'] -> items['9']. The benchmark harness (github.com/EQ-bench/creative-writing-bench, utils/api.py) unconditionally sends `payload["reasoning"]={"enabled": False}` for every OpenRouter-routed model, and its think-tag stripping only fires when a response contains both an opening `<think>` and a closing `</think>`; both leaked rows contain the closing tag with no opening tag, which is exactly why the harness's own stripping logic let them through -- the reasoning-suppression request was not honored end-to-end for this one cloaked, backend-unknown slug, and the resulting malformed completion reached both scoring pipelines undetected by a frontier judge model. Elsewhere, a whole-file grep of both JSON files finds no provider, finish_reason, or chat-template-token fields -- the only 8 hits for the string "provider" are the English word inside story prose (e.g., "healthcare provider") -- and all 8 of the cloaked OpenRouter model slugs named for this benchmark (quasar-alpha, optimus-alpha, cypher-alpha:free, horizon-alpha, horizon-beta, sherlock-dash-alpha, pony-alpha, hunter-alpha) appear as test_model values. generation_failed=true totals exactly 53 of 10,264 rows, broken down as gemma-3-4b-it:free 6/88, cypher-alpha:free 5/96, and claude-3.5-haiku 8/96; but generation_failed is purely a client-side "<500 characters after 3 retries" signal (core/conversation.py), indistinguishable from an ordinary short or refused output using only what's published, so it remains suggestive rather than confirmed evidence of routing-induced corruption.

Hereditary Traits Distillation (Open Dis.text: The project's raw generation log, results/or_generations.jsonl (1440 rows), contains 360 rows that are hard API failures rather than model output: openai/gpt-oss-120b returns "Reasoning is mandatory" on all 180 of its rows, and allenai/olmo-3-32b-think returns "404 No endpoints found" on all 180 of its rows, a count results/or_run.log logs independently ("wrote 1440 assistant turns -> .../or_generations.jsonl (360 errored)"). reports/depression_rejection_protocol.md and reports/gemini_flash_nothink.md disclose and exclude these API-failure rows from the reported analysis; the latter's reasoning-token table, showing 0 tokens versus 717-882 tokens, is a legitimate empirical check that a "nothink" setting suppressed reasoning as intended rather than a sign of routing or quantization drift. reports/report_18_qwen_base_not_clean_floor/README.md retracts an earlier claim that the Qwen base model ran on a clean quantization floor. The interactive trace viewer post_figures/blackmail_nemotron_traces.html, linked alongside the repo from the project's AlignmentForum post, embeds JSON for 34 agentic-misalignment transcripts tagged 6 "openrouter" and 28 "tinker" for provider, with coherent multi-paragraph reasoning text and zero special-token or U+FFFD (replacement-character) artifacts. In chinese_censorship_eval/results/, the per-model files carry their real provider-prefixed names — qwen_qwen3.5-397b-a17b.jsonl, moonshotai_kimi-k2.7-code.jsonl, z-ai_glm-5.2.jsonl — and roughly 21,000 additional rows across them are likewise free of token-soup or special-token corruption. The openrouter/tinker tag used throughout is only a coarse transport label, though, so this evidence rules out corruption at that granularity without distinguishing among OpenRouter's own sub-backends.

Inspect Evals (UK AISI eval collection).text: The published output for this OpenRouter-critical path (persistbench's judge model kimi-k2-thinking scoring llama-3.3-70b-instruct and gpt-oss-120b) is exactly two ~7KB JSON files and a report.md; both JSON files were opened directly, not inferred from a directory listing. They are Inspect AI eval-log headers with no 'samples' array whatsoever: just an aggregate failure_rate score per model, total/reasoning token counts, timing, and the model identifier string (e.g. "openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"). There is no per-sample provider or generation-id field (F2 not checkable), no completion text to inspect for token soup (F1) or special-token litter (F4), no finish_reason or answer-field data (F3), and no reasoning-field per-row structure (F5). The README treats logs as a local-only artifact viewed via `inspect view`, not something the project releases. This is the textbook nothing_checkable_released case: aggregate numbers only, matched against the paper's reference values, with no raw generation data underlying either number.
Inspect Evals (UK AISI eval collection).what_is_published: For the specific OpenRouter-served path in question (persistbench), the repo publishes only two small JSON files and a markdown report, all aggregate: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_meta-llama_llama-3.3-70b-instruct.json (7188 bytes) and .../2026-05-22_openrouter_openai_gpt-oss-120b.json (7168 bytes), plus src/inspect_evals/persistbench/report.md. Confirmed by downloading and parsing both JSON files directly (not just via summarizer): top-level keys are version/status/eval/plan/results/stats/invalidated/tags/metadata — there is no 'samples' key at all. 'results' contains only the scored aggregate failure_rate metric (50.0 for llama-3.3-70b-instruct, 16.0 for gpt-oss-120b) and sample counts (100/100); 'stats.model_usage' contains only summed input/output/reasoning token counts per model string, no per-sample breakdown. report.md gives the same two failure-rate numbers plus the paper's reference values/CIs, run date, versions, repo commit, total token count, and cost (~$1.02), with a local-only reproduction command. The persistbench README explicitly frames logs as something you generate and view locally via `inspect view`, not something hosted or released. The GitHub API directory listing confirms these are the only two files present (no .eval file, no per-sample logs, no other result subdirectories — persistbench_cross_domain and persistbench_sycophancy have no results published at all).

JudgeArena (OpenEuroLLM).text: Nothing a skeptical reader could independently re-derive was published. The repository is pure tooling and source code, with no committed run outputs, no per-sample score files, no leaderboard, and no HuggingFace or Zenodo dataset, despite the paper's stated intent to release "a dataset containing all annotations." The paper's own experiments were run with locally-hosted vLLM, not OpenRouter, and the paper is explicit that "All models with more than 9B parameters are quantized with half-precision" — a disclosed methodological choice, not a hidden fingerprint, and irrelevant to OpenRouter routing since OpenRouter is a backend option only in the current, later generalized version of the tool, not in the paper's reported runs. With no raw completions, provider metadata, or transcripts released anywhere, none of the fingerprint checks (F1-F11) are executable; only an F16-style paperwork observation applies (an unlinked data-release promise), which per the rubric is not evidence of corruption.
JudgeArena (OpenEuroLLM).what_is_published: The GitHub repo (github.com/OpenEuroLLM/JudgeArena, top-level listing checked via `gh api repos/OpenEuroLLM/JudgeArena/contents`) contains only source code: .github, configs/ (alpaca_eval.yaml, elo_comparia.yaml — config templates, not results), judgearena/ (package code), scripts/, slurmpilot_scripts/, tests/, README.md, TODOs.md. There is no results/, outputs/, logs/, runs/, transcripts/, or trajectories/ directory, no committed .jsonl of raw generations (confirmed via GitHub code search for *.jsonl in the repo — zero hits), no tags, and no releases. The associated paper (arXiv 2501.17178, "Tuning LLM Judge Design Decisions for 1/1000 of the Cost") ends with "We will release the code to reproduce our results, along with a dataset containing all annotations" but gives no URL/DOI, and no corresponding HuggingFace dataset or Zenodo record was found under the OpenEuroLLM org (checked huggingface.co/OpenEuroLLM — 25 datasets listed, none named for JudgeArena/arena-hard/alpaca-eval annotations) or as a GitHub release/tag on the repo. The paper's appendix (Figs. 9-10) shows two hand-picked illustrative judge-prompt/output examples, not a released corpus.

METR RE-Bench task suite (inspect-tasks-.text: The inspect-tasks-public repo itself contains only harness and task-definition code: a GitHub API tree listing confirms no results/, logs/, or transcripts/ directories exist there, and no per-run data ships with the repo. The associated paper and its predecessor harness (github.com/METR/RE-Bench) claim a real release: METR's blog post explicitly links per-run pages at transcripts.metr.org for RE-Bench episodes, including one captioned "RL training script improvement" (run #1511911269) that maps to the nanogpt_chat_rl / "Finetune GPT-2 for QA with RL" task — the only RL task in the suite. That transcript page returns only its SPA shell on a direct fetch ("Vivaria Runs" with an empty #root div, since it is a JS single-page app); a JS-rendering proxy (r.jina.ai) returns "Seems the API is down" and repeated "Failed to fetch" errors, i.e. the page loads but its backing API is not currently serving content; and direct probes of plausible API paths (api/getRun, api/runs/<id>, etc.) all return HTTP 403. No HuggingFace dataset specific to RE-Bench exists; a search surfaces only an unrelated, empty METR "malt-transcripts-public" dataset. Raw per-run transcripts were genuinely published and specifically linked by METR, but their content is not retrievable through the page, the rendering proxy, or the API, and whether those transcripts capture the separate LLM-judge scoring subprocess (the meta-llama/llama-3-8b-instruct win-rate calls) as opposed to only the agent's ML-engineering actions remains unknown. No positive or negative claim about fingerprints in that data follows from this: the gap is one of access to data that was genuinely published, not an absence of published data, so it does not collapse to nothing_checkable_released.

Multi-hop / no-CoT latent reasoning expe.text: This project's own .gitignore explicitly excludes /caches/ (the raw OpenRouter/Anthropic response cache written by response_cache.py — where any provider id, finish_reason, or reasoning-field content would live), /eval_results/*.json (per-sample score files), and /data/ from version control, and the write-up's appendix on the sister repo compose_facts says outright "I've removed the datasets... PLEASE DON'T PUBLICLY POST THIS DATASET." Nothing a skeptical reader could grep for a provider key, count finish_reason=='length', or eyeball for token-soup/special-token litter has ever been released — only PNG plots and prose accuracy percentages, plus prompt text (not completions) in the appendices. No HuggingFace dataset, Zenodo artifact, GitHub release, or per-sample log is committed in either rgreenblatt/multi_hop or rgreenblatt/compose_facts, a conclusion drawn from the full recursive git trees of both repositories rather than from top-level directory listings; neither repo has any releases. This is the expected default outcome, not a strained one: the author was transparent about the OpenRouter-prefill hack and its risk in prose (footnote ^notesonreason), but transparency about the risk is not the same as publishing checkable data, and none was published.

Nous Research — Autoreason (self-refinem.text: github.com/NousResearch/autoreason contains 9,413 git-tracked files (per `git ls-files`), including experiments/v2/results_small_vs_big/{gtm,pitch,policy}/{haiku35_single,...}/final_output.md and per-pass critic.md/version_a.md/version_b.md/version_ab.md/history.json, 7 judge_N_raw.txt files plus results.json Borda tallies under each task's eval/ dir, results_code_{haiku,s46,haiku45,best_of_n}/ with per-problem JSON, a paper/autoreason.tex with a Reproducibility Details appendix (app:repro, lines 1095-1120), and all 62 .py scripts. experiments/v2/results_small_vs_big/gtm/haiku35_single/final_output.md contains genuine decode-level word-fusion corruption ("DevOToolil", "ered freemiumrum", "MILEST:ONES") ending in a hallucinated "Would Human: you elaborate..." turn; run_single_pass() writes response.choices[0].message.content unmodified, so this is not a harness string-splicing bug. The sibling pitch/haiku35_single and policy/haiku35_single outputs, produced by the same code path and the same model string (openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku from CHEAP_MODELS), are fully coherent end to end, making the corruption an isolated single-call failure rather than a systemic template or parsing defect. gtm/eval/results.json records a Borda score of 7 out of 35 and 0 first-place votes for haiku35_single. In flash_autoreason, pass_06/version_b.md and pass_07/version_a.md are byte-identical, both end with an unclosed ```text fence, and both cut off mid-sentence at "assign ownership to specific team members," consistent with the script's MAX_TOKENS=4096; no script anywhere logs usage or finish_reason (a grep across all 62 .py files turns up only two hits, both on the unrelated phrase "usage-based pricing," not response metadata), so neither the truncation's cause nor any provider mixing behind it can be determined from what's published. The paper's Reproducibility Details appendix also diverges from the code it describes: run_code_haiku45.py calls anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 directly, with no OpenRouter or api_base wiring, where the paper states "claude-3-5-haiku-latest via OpenRouter," and run_small_vs_big.py routes its gemini_flash condition through openrouter/google/gemini-2.0-flash-001 where the paper states "via Google AI." Neither the garbling nor the truncation is shown to be caused specifically by quantization, and the appendix mismatches read as documentation inconsistencies rather than proof of result corruption.

OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Traine.text: OpenPipe ART's teacher-distillation example is a toy demo, not a research artifact: it sends a single hardcoded prompt to `z-ai/glm-5` over OpenRouter and immediately consumes the response in memory to build one training trajectory, printing only a 500-character preview to stdout. Nothing about this call — no transcript, no provider/generation-id metadata, no reasoning field, no benchmark row — is persisted or published anywhere in the repo. The one committed JSONL file in the same directory belongs to an unrelated, separate example script and has no connection to OpenRouter or GLM-5, confirmed by reading both files directly rather than inferring from a directory listing. No appendix, HuggingFace dataset, or run log exists to check for token soup, provider mixing, truncation, or template litter, because the pipeline that would produce such evidence discards its output after a single print statement. This is the expected/default case: a code example, not an evaluation whose results get published.
OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Traine.what_is_published: Nothing tied to the OpenRouter/GLM-5 call. The relevant code is `dev/sft/distillation.py` (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenPipe/ART/main/dev/sft/distillation.py), a 68-line demo script that sends exactly one hardcoded prompt ("Explain the concept of recursion in programming with a simple example.") to `teacher_client.chat.completions.create(model="z-ai/glm-5", ...)` via `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"`. The teacher response is only printed to stdout truncated to 500 chars (`print(f"Teacher response ({len(teacher_response)} chars):\n{teacher_response[:500]}...")`) and held in memory to build a single `art.Trajectory` for an SFT training call — it is never written to disk, logged, or committed anywhere in the repo. The `dev/sft/dataset.jsonl` file sitting in the same directory (10 lines, PII-redaction training examples with `messages`/`response_format`/`split` fields) belongs to the separate `sft-from-file.py` example and has no connection to OpenRouter or GLM-5 — confirmed by reading both files directly. `assets/benchmarks/*` (2048, codenames, email_agent, etc.) contain only SVG plots (e.g. `assets/benchmarks/2048/accuracy-comparison.svg`), no per-sample data. A GitHub code search for "openrouter" and "glm-5" scoped to the repo turned up nothing beyond this one file.
OpenPipe ART (Agent Reinforcement Traine.evidence: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenPipe/ART/main/dev/sft/distillation.py (full file read); https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenPipe/ART/main/dev/sft/dataset.jsonl (10 lines, unrelated PII-redaction data, read via curl); gh api repos/OpenPipe/ART/contents/assets/benchmarks/2048 -> only .svg files; gh api search/code -f q='openrouter+repo:OpenPipe/ART' and 'glm-5+repo:OpenPipe/ART' -> no hits beyond distillation.py.

Prompt Framing Changes LLM Performance (.text: Nothing published here lets a reader check serving integrity: no raw completions, no provider or generation-id metadata, and no judge transcripts. tables/results_full.csv (github.com/kmerkelbach/llm-request-tone/blob/main/tables/results_full.csv), opened directly rather than inferred from a directory listing, holds only aggregated scores (model, benchmark, scenario, metric, value, stderr) and no per-sample text. The repo's own .gitignore files (root: `results`; src/.gitignore: `results_*.json`, `samples_*.jsonl`) show the lm-eval per-sample outputs were kept local and never committed, and the author's SORRY-Bench fork gitignores `data/` wholesale, leaving only empty `.dummy` placeholders where model answers and gpt-oss-120b judge verdicts would sit. The LessWrong post itself, checked directly rather than assumed, contains no appendix and no example transcripts. No F1–F11 check is possible: nothing textual, no provider field, no reasoning-field structure, and no finish_reason data exist in what was released. The one applicable observation is F16 (paperwork tells): the post names gpt-oss-120b as judge and OpenRouter as backend but never names a provider, precision, or snapshot date for any of the six evaluated models, consistent with — but not proof of — the code-level routing risk already flagged. This is the expected default outcome, not a strained one.
Prompt Framing Changes LLM Performance (.what_is_published: Only aggregate benchmark tables. The repo (github.com/kmerkelbach/llm-request-tone) commits tables/results_full.csv (1140 rows of model/benchmark/scenario/metric/value/stderr — no completion text, no per-sample rows) plus derived mean/median/min/max CSV+MD rollups under tables/lm-eval/ and tables/sorry_bench/, and tables/stderr_analysis.md (a 4-row relative-SE summary). The LessWrong writeup contains only prose and these same summary tables, no appendix transcripts or example completions. Critically, both repos this project depends on explicitly exclude raw generations from version control: llm-request-tone's top-level .gitignore lists `results` and its src/.gitignore lists `results_*.json` / `samples_*.jsonl` (lm-eval-harness's per-sample output files); the author's SORRY-Bench fork (kmerkelbach/sorry-bench, branch feature/llm-tone) gitignores `data` entirely and ships only `.dummy` placeholder files in data/sorry_bench/model_answer/ and model_judgment/ where actual generations and judge verdicts would live.

Redwood Research — BashArena (basharena_.text: Two Docent collections tied to the BashArena results contain agent_run transcripts with verifiable model identity: run d4661ec2 in collection 0fe149a0 and run a34e95c7 in collection fc1f9a0c. Raw message-blob substring counts on these transcripts show 51 occurrences of "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929" in the honest run and 9 in the attack run, with zero hits for any openrouter, gpt-oss, or kimi string, zero special-token artifacts (<|im_start|>, <|eot_id|>), and zero U+FFFD replacement characters in either transcript. The agent_runs schema itself carries no model or provider metadata field, only task and score metadata, so model identity is recoverable only by reading the raw message JSON rather than from any summary column. The five remaining dashboards follow the same BashBench2_auto_usefulness_protocol_wrapper_{honest,attack}_<hash> task_id naming pattern. The paper's line that GPT-5, GPT-5-nano, and Kimi-K2 never triggered the firewall side task in 100 trajectories each stands as a single aggregate sentence with no dashboard link or transcript backing that slice.

Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight (Eng.text: No systematic raw-output dataset was released — no per-sample completions, no provider/generation-id fields, no finish_reason or reasoning-field data — so F2/F3/F5/F6/F7/F11 cannot be checked at all. Three curated illustrative excerpts of real model dialogue/code do appear inside the PDF itself, confirmed via pdftotext -layout rather than inferred from image captions: the Mafia snippet in Fig. 10 ("Emma (Villager): Okay, let's play this out. If I were Mafia, and Rachel called me out for being quiet, I'd probably try to look super cooperative..."), the Wargames snippet in Fig. 6, and the backdoor code in Listing 1. All three read as coherent, topical, grammatical text, with no token soup, no special/chat-template tokens, no U+FFFD, and no truncation signatures — a genuinely clean read of what exists. That sample is extremely thin and author-selected, however: three snippets chosen to showcase "cool strategies," not a random draw, against what must be thousands of API calls across 4 games x 13 models x a 2-month collection window, so it cannot certify the bulk of unreleased transcripts one way or the other. Combined with the paperwork-tell context already flagged at the code level — bare OpenRouter slugs, no provider/precision disclosed (F16) — the honest read is inconclusive rather than checked_clean.
Scaling Laws For Scalable Oversight (Eng.what_is_published: The GitHub repo (subhashk01/oversight-scaling-laws) contains only code (debate/, mafia/, backdoor-code/, wargames/, elo/, theory/) plus prompt *templates* (backdoor-code/data/prompts/{guard/original.txt, houdini/system}) and one aggregate CSV (elo/llm_elos.csv, Chatbot-Arena Elo reference numbers). There is no results/, outputs/, logs/, transcripts/ directory, no HuggingFace/Zenodo dataset, and the two committed notebooks (elo/all_analysis.ipynb, debate/debate_extract_json.ipynb) retain only win-rate matrices, Elo-fit printouts, and matplotlib PNGs as cell outputs — no raw per-sample completions or provider metadata anywhere. The paper (arXiv:2504.18530v3) itself embeds three small illustrative transcript/code excerpts as real extractable text: Figure 10 (Mafia, all players gemini-2.0-flash-001), Figure 6 (Wargames, Houdini/Guard = GPT-4o, Narrator = Gemini-2.0-Flash), and Listing 1 (a backdoor inserted by ChatGPT-4o on APPS #166). Seer (interpretability agent harness) /.text: Twenty of the 25 notebooks in the companion repo actually call OpenRouter target models (china_elicit_qwen, kimi_investigation, kimi_investigation_attacker, and quote_harry_potter_llama, each run five times), and every one of them sets extra_body={"provider":{"order":[provider],"allow_fallbacks":False}} with a single named provider — siliconflow/fp8, inference-net, or, for the one documented run3 override, novita/fp8 — matching the YAML configs verbatim. The run3 markdown cell's quote describing that override is exact and unaltered. Across all 25 notebooks, grep for the special tokens <|eot_id|>, <|im_start|>, <|endoftext|>, and raw U+FFFD bytes returns zero hits, and a check for runs of five or more repeated words finds none either. In run3's Qwen notebook, the 12 full ChatCompletion reprs show the model field uniformly as 'qwen/qwen3-32b', and every call with finish_reason='length' has completion_tokens exactly equal to the max_tokens=1000 cap — the stop-terminated calls end at 439, 423, 415, 52, and 63 tokens, while all five length-terminated calls land at exactly 1000 — the signature of hitting a declared cap rather than silent under-cap truncation. The remaining 5 notebooks, one user_preference run per task set, use a completely different backend (scribe.modal.InterpClient with direct tokenizer and model.generate calls) and contain no occurrences of allow_fallbacks, siliconflow, or inference-net, so they fall outside the scope of the provider-pinning question rather than showing unpinned routing. Provider pinning is consistent across 20 of the 25 notebooks — four of five task types in each of the five runs — not across all 25.

ctfish (Palisade Research — \"Demonstrat.text: For deepseek/deepseek-r1 in variant=baseline, task_md5=a22c2dfeb74cf70726f15a3dcb3e7b48, exactly four runs — timestamped 2025-01-23T06:48, 08:27, 08:38, and 08:49 UTC, with final_step values 4, 5, 3, and 1 — contain a literal `<think>...</think>` tag inline in `.content`, while runs immediately before and after on the same day and same task never do; across all 128 deepseek-r1 runs in the file, these four (13 of 2554 entries) are the only ones that ever show `<think>`. This lines up with OpenRouter's own documented behavior for DeepSeek-R1, which sometimes inlines reasoning into `content` and sometimes returns it in a separate field that the harness never captures. Separately, the qwen/qwq-32b-preview run at 2025-01-21T23:23:27 contains a literal `<|im_start|>\nHuman:` chat-template leak followed by a hallucinated "Final Solution" turn — the only place in the entire 50MB runs.json where `<|im_start|>`, `<|eot_id|>`, or `<|endoftext|>` appears. player.py's OpenRouter client (base_url=openrouter.ai, no extra_body provider pin) and its `reply = result.choices[0].message.content` extraction are unchanged across the relevant window, so neither anomaly traces to a harness-code change. The four anomalous runs' final entries are complete, well-formed PLAN/REMEMBER blocks rather than truncations cut off mid-clause with an empty answer field, and equally low final_step values recur throughout the rest of the 128-run population on unrelated days with no `<think>` tags present at all — consistent with the paper's own appendix admission that "the inference API was unstable" and runs were cut short at the session level, rather than with per-completion truncation.

diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning /.text: No raw generation ever surfaces to a reader: not the agent's own investigation transcripts, not a judge's hypothesis-grade JSON, not a coherence-grader verdict on a real steered sample. The pipeline that would have produced checkable per-sample data exists (narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/agent_grades/data_preprocessing.py, which parses real `hypothesis_grade_*.json` files with `score` and `grader_model_id` fields — exactly the F2-shaped artifact one would want), but its input directory lives only on the authors' local/cluster filesystem and was never committed or mirrored to HF/Zenodo. The paper does name the serving path plainly — "the agents are based on openai/gpt-5 with default settings (thinking strength medium) as hosted by openrouter.ai" (Appendix C.4) — which is an F16 paperwork disclosure (named model + named router, no provider/precision/snapshot pin), not evidence either way. Given that no transcripts, per-sample scores, or provider metadata were released in any form that could be opened and checked, this is a clean nothing_checkable_released rather than an inconclusive or a fingerprint claim.
diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning /.what_is_published: Nothing beyond aggregate numbers and hand-authored prompt templates. Checked three places: (1) GitHub repo science-of-finetuning/diffing-toolkit — contains only code. narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/{agent_grader_interactions,agent_grades}/data_preprocessing.py is the actual pipeline that reads real per-run judge output (`hypothesis_grade_*.json` with `score`/`grader_model_id`/`run_idx`), but it does so from a LOCAL, unpublished directory (`_results_root_from_cfg` builds a path from `cfg.diffing.results_dir` and asserts `root.exists()`) — that directory is not committed to the repo, not on the HF org, and not linked anywhere. (2) The arXiv paper (2510.13900v3, 67pp, fetched as PDF and grepped in full) reports only aggregate bar/deviation charts (Fig. 3, 4, 24-26) and prose like "Analysis of agent traces reveals that agents occasionally identify the correct pattern..." (p.42) without reproducing a single real transcript. Appendix C.4/Prompts 15-21 give the system prompts verbatim, but every "example" inside them (the clinical-medication-counseling FINAL string, the Patchscope scale-selection reasoning) is explicitly a hand-authored illustrative template ("Examples of individual agent turns:"), not a logged output from an actual run. (3) The HuggingFace org science-of-finetuning (70 models, 107 datasets) hosts only SAE/crosscoder activation statistics and synthetic-document finetuning-data datasets — no agent trajectories, hypothesis grades, or judge transcripts. No Zenodo artifact found. GitHub issues contain no complaints about garbled/gibberish output or provider variance.

lighteval — Swiss-Legal / LEXam LLM-as-j.text: The public Hugging Face bucket hf://buckets/joelniklaus/SwissLegalEvals — listable and downloadable with no auth token — holds per-model, per-task lighteval parquet files whose contents show a sharp, provider-specific failure in the SLDS judge task. In `details_slds:de_de`, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct returns empty completions for 101 of 207 rows (48.8%), and the empty rate is a clean step function in prompt length rather than a gradual decline: 1.9% under roughly 20k query characters, 93.2% between 20k and 30k, and 100% above 30k. `details_slds:it_it` shows the same pattern at smaller scale, 9 of 12 rows empty. The identical 207 (de_de) and 12 (it_it) prompts, matched by document id — including the single longest queries at 78,875 and 70,921 characters — produce zero empty completions from DeepSeek-V4-Pro and NVIDIA-Nemotron served on the same HF-Inference-Providers routing layer; `generation_size` (32768) and `stop_sequences` are constant across empty and non-empty rows, so a per-sample max_tokens setting is not the cause, and the `reasonings` field is empty for all 207 Llama rows, never populated, meaning the failure is empty-from-the-start rather than a chain-of-thought that trails off partway through. Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct has a genuine 128k-token context window, so a hard cutoff at roughly 20-30k characters — about 150,000 tokens short of that limit — points to the effective context window or token budget of the specific novita endpoint serving the model, not to the model's architecture. The judge scores every empty completion 0, and REPORT.md's SLDS scores reflect that gap directly (Llama 16.10 vs. Nemotron 55.13, Kimi 49.81, DeepSeek-V4-Pro 50.49), while its "Known limitations" section names only DeepSeek self-preference bias, the 0-100/0-1 composite-score scale mix, and Hy-MT2 being translation-only — nothing about empty completions — so the failure is absorbed into a "translation specialist" narrative rather than diagnosed. The bucket runs on HF Inference Providers with named backends (novita) rather than OpenRouter's own unpinned router, so this evidences the broader provider-routing/context-mismatch risk class rather than an OpenRouter-specific finding.

safety-tooling (safety-research org shar.text: safety-tooling is infrastructure (an inference-API library), not a paper or benchmark run, and it publishes nothing a reader could check for serving-quality fingerprints. The full repo tree, README, .gitignore, the OpenRouter test file, both example notebooks, and the linked Zenodo record were opened directly, not inferred from a directory listing. No generations, transcripts, per-sample logs, or leaderboard rows exist anywhere in what this project released — the repo's own .gitignore excludes the exact directories (`.cache`, `logs/`, `.prompt_history`) where model I/O would land, the OpenRouter unit tests are skipped without a live key and don't persist output, the example notebooks have empty output cells, and the Zenodo DOI is a 147 kB source-code zip, not a data archive. This is the expected default for a tooling repo: there is simply no artifact to inspect, so no fingerprint from the catalogue (F1-F17) applies in either direction.
safety-tooling (safety-research org shar.what_is_published: safety-tooling is a shared inference-library codebase, not a paper or eval with results. It publishes only source code (github.com/safety-research/safety-tooling) plus a Zenodo software snapshot (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.15363603, a 147.2 kB zip of the v1.0.0 tag — confirmed via WebFetch to be "Software" resource type, not a dataset). There is no results/, outputs/, logs/, or transcripts/ directory in the repo tree (checked via `git/trees/main?recursive=1`); the .gitignore explicitly excludes `.cache`, `logs/`, and `.prompt_history` (the exact directories the README says model I/O would land in — README.md line 222: prompt logging is "off by default" and writes to `$exp_dir/prompt_history`, which is gitignored). The only OpenRouter-touching artifacts in the repo are source (`safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py`) and a test file (`tests/test_other_apis.py`, lines 28-149) whose OpenRouter tests are `@pytest.mark.skipif` when `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` is absent and, even when run, produce no persisted/committed output. The two example notebooks (`examples/inference_api/inference_api.ipynb`, `vllm_api.ipynb`) contain template code with empty `outputs: []` on every cell — never executed-and-saved, and neither references OpenRouter anyway.

Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivize.text: EXAMPLES.md was read in full (all 570 lines) and grepped for provider/generation-id keys, special tokens, and finish_reason/truncation markers; none appear. Every completion present — all from google/gemini-3-pro-preview or openai/gpt-5, e.g. the Frank Munn, John Lewis, KLM/Pan Am, Round Table Pizza, and D&D-lore examples — is coherent, on-topic, natural prose with no token soup (F1), no special-token litter or U+FFFD (F4), no empty-answer/length-truncation pattern (F3), and no reasoning field at all to check for shape drift (F5), consistent with short direct-answer SimpleQA completions rather than CoT. This is a genuinely clean read of what's there, but it does not settle the serving-integrity question: the file is a ~60-completion illustrative slice of the 17,304 total, covering only 2 of the 4 models, with no claude-opus-4.5 or grok-4 examples appearing anywhere in it. lm.py's full source was read directly, not inferred from a listing, and on no code path does it request or store a provider/generation-id field, making F2 — the one fingerprint that could actually prove or disprove mixed backends — structurally unrecoverable from anything this project could ever publish, at any sample size. The clean read is real but thin and partial; the decisive check is permanently unavailable.
Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivize.what_is_published: The repo (github.com/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments) contains only code (experiment.ipynb, lm.py) plus one data file: EXAMPLES.md (570 lines, 29KB). No results/, outputs/, logs/, or transcripts/ directory; no HuggingFace or Zenodo dataset; no per-sample score file; the SimpleQA test set itself is downloaded at runtime from OpenAI's own blob storage, not committed. EXAMPLES.md is a real, verbatim slice of the pipeline's output: for each of the 8 baseline/mitigated scoring-outcome buckets (TIE/WIN/LOSS, spanning the reported ns of 7276, 5015, 1652, 1200, 1191, 609, 160, 72, 54, 41, 34 examples), it shows 2 baseline completions plus 1 full "consistency mitigation" trace (two sampled responses + the judge's verdict + final answer). That is roughly 60 individual model completions out of the 17,304 = 4 models x 4,326 questions actually run. README.md confirms no other data artifact exists ("See some Examples" links only to this file) and gives no data-availability statement.
Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivize.evidence: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments/main/EXAMPLES.md (read in full, 570 lines) — grep for `<|...|>|<think>|im_start|eot_id|endoftext|U+FFFD`, `provider|generation.id|gen-`, and `finish_reason|truncat|max_tokens` all returned zero matches. https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments/main/lm.py (read in full, 330 lines) — `_generate()` extracts only `completion["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]` (and, in with_usage mode, `usage` minus `is_byok`/`prompt_tokens_details`/`cost_details`); no `provider` or generation-id field is ever read or cached. https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments/main/README.md confirms EXAMPLES.md is the only released data artifact ("See some Examples") and the SimpleQA CSV is downloaded, not committed. GitHub API listing of repo root (api.github.com/repos/openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments/contents/) shows only .gitignore, EXAMPLES.md, LICENSE, README.md, experiment.ipynb, figures/, lm.py — no results/logs/transcripts directory.

Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute).text: Inspect AI is infrastructure other people run their own evals with, not itself a study that produced a results set. The main repo, the docs site, and the companion inspect_evals repo (eval task collection) contain no raw generations: none of the three publishes committed run logs, sample .eval files, transcripts, per-sample score files, or a leaderboard. The `logs/` mentions that appear are explicitly local output a user's own `inspect eval` invocation creates, not something UKGovernmentBEIS ships. The eval-logs documentation page describes the log/transcript format in prose and screenshots but links no actual sample file. Since the project has never published a dataset of its own OpenRouter-routed completions, no artifact exists for a skeptical reader to inspect for provider mixing, token soup, truncation, or any other fingerprint. The code-level "at_risk" finding remains a code-review concern only, with no observational evidence for or against it in anything this project has released.
Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute).what_is_published: Nothing beyond documentation and code. Inspect AI (github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai) is an evaluation-harness framework/library, not a study with results. Its repo contains src/, docs/ (Quarto site at inspect.aisi.org.uk), examples/, and tests/ — no results/, logs/, or transcripts/ directory of real run outputs. The companion UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals repo (200+ pre-built eval task implementations) likewise ships only task code, not per-sample outputs; its logs/ reference in the README is documented as a directory a user's own `inspect eval` run creates locally, not a committed artifact. The docs' eval-logs page describes the `.eval` log format and log viewer only via prose/screenshots — no downloadable sample `.eval` file with real model completions, reasoning traces, or provider metadata is linked. inspect.aisi.org.uk/evals/ is a catalogue of available tasks, not a leaderboard with scores. No paper, no HuggingFace dataset, no Zenodo artifact, no leaderboard exists for this project.
Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute).evidence: Checked: github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai (repo structure: src/, docs/, examples/, tests/, scripts/, design/ — no results/logs/transcripts dir); github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals (src/, docs/, tests/, register/, analysis/ — no committed run outputs; README's "logs/" reference is for user-generated local output via `inspect view`); inspect.aisi.org.uk/evals/ (catalogue page, no scores/leaderboard); inspect.aisi.org.uk/eval-logs.html (format documentation only, no downloadable/linked sample .eval file with real completions).

MathArena (ETH Zurich SRI Lab).text: Across MathArena's published raw-generation data, microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus:free is the only one of 159 model configs in the eth-sri/matharena repo tagged ":free," and its outputs hit output_tokens==31000 on exactly 25 of 120 AIME 2025 rows and 38 of 120 HMMT Feb 2025 rows, with the maximum output length capped at 31000 in both datasets despite the config's max_tokens: 31500 — a 500-token shortfall that repeats identically across two independent competitions. Row problem_idx=14, idx_answer=1 shows this directly: the assistant's response text cuts off mid-sentence at "...We want this to be a perfect square of something of," with no closing and no boxed answer, and the recorded parsed_answer of 66248 (gold_answer 60, correct False) is simply the coefficient lifted from the truncated line "147196 - 66248√3" — a value outside AIME's valid 0-999 answer range, the signature of truncation followed by garbage answer-extraction rather than a reasoning error. The same two files show comparison models running well within their configured limits: Llama-4-Maverick tops out at 2080/3025 output tokens against a configured 32000, and Kimi K2 Thinking at 115084/91400 against a configured 256000, with Kimi's assistant-turn structure a clean Counter of {('cot','response'): 118, ('cot','response','cot','response'): 2} across all 120 rows in both datasets. Searching all three models' all_messages text for special tokens (<|eot_id|>, <|im_start|>, </s>, <|endoftext|>), the U+FFFD replacement character, and literal think-tags returns zero hits. No file in the repo — not any config, not api_client.py — hardcodes a 31000-token constant anywhere, so the ceiling does not originate in the harness itself. Nor does any config or code path set a provider-routing block for any model, including Phi-4: no provider: key, no quantization or endpoint filter, no explicit pin — requests to OpenRouter carry only model, max_tokens, temperature, and top_p, leaving this free-tier model's routing fully default and unpinned. That absence is itself the exposure: an unpinned free-tier OpenRouter route appears to impose a completion-length wall well below the declared max_tokens, corrupting a nontrivial share of scored answers through truncation followed by nonsense answer-extraction.

OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 D&B) — mm_agents/{.text: xlangai/ubuntu_osworld_verified_trajs on HuggingFace hosts results_maestro.zip (2,887,884,643 bytes, matching the claimed ~2.9GB) and results-vlaa-gui.zip (4,231,346,306 bytes, matching the claimed ~4.2GB), both range-readable. results_maestro.zip contains exactly 409 state/commands.json files (one per task run) under results_maestro/runtime/awsrun_*/*/state/, each with per-step pre_screenshot_analysis, gate_checks.json, subtasks.json, and controller_state.json; results-vlaa-gui.zip contains exactly 357 traj.jsonl files with a per-step executor_plan field. No os_symphony-named archive or path appears anywhere in the dataset's file listing, and mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json on GitHub sets planner_role, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, supplement_role, and the other roles to provider="openrouter", model_name="openai/o3". Sampled files — including .../20250908_074353_000/state/commands.json, .../20250908_080258_230/state/commands.json, and the vlaa-gui traj.jsonl files under UUID paths 0810415c-... and 26660ad1-... — hold coherent, unaltered text (e.g. the "Google Chrome window is open and maximized" pre_screenshot_analysis line), with the only "provider" string hits being unrelated false positives: a Jekyll config's "analytics:\n  provider: false" and a Speedtest.net "GTT provider" ISP description embedded in a screenshot analysis. A further sample of 8 additional commands.json files turned up zero special-token hits and zero provider/openrouter string hits, so no chat-template or special-token leakage appears anywhere sampled. Neither export format (commands.json, traj.jsonl, runtime.log) carries provider/generation-id, finish_reason, or token-count fields anywhere sampled: a runtime.log opened for the vlaa-gui "claude" backbone run logs only a Bedrock-style model string (global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-5-20251101-v1:0), not an OpenRouter identifier. So the published trajectories read as coherent and free of tokenizer/template leakage, but the release carries no data of any kind on which provider actually served the requests.

OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin).text: Per-config metadata.json files for the qwen-2.5-72b-instruct and llama-3.1-70b-instruct runs specify `"model": "openrouter/qwen/qwen-2.5-72b-instruct"` and `"openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct"`, both with `base_url: null` and no provider field, indicating unpinned OpenRouter routing at the config level (F16). In the 59MB qwen output.jsonl, instance sympy__sympy-19254 contains a 3514-character agent message of incoherent `the`/`of`/`中`/`<|endoftext|>` fused text, and sympy__sympy-20049 contains a 1212-character agent message that is almost entirely repeated `<|endoftext|>`. Grepping literal `<|...|>` tokens across this file returns 234 hits, all confined to these same two instances, versus zero hits across two pinned non-OpenRouter negative-control logs of 300 instances each (Fireworks-direct llama-v3p1-70b and DeepSeek-direct deepseek-chat) — topically unrelated word salad (F1) plus literal EOS-token litter surviving into prose (F4), not documented code-switching. The `empty_generation: true` field is not distinctive: it fires on 10-29% of instances in every file checked, including both pinned negative controls, so it carries no diagnostic weight on its own. Pure repetition loops without special tokens, such as a 24-line "static\nstatic\n..." loop, also occur in the DeepSeek-direct negative control, so repetition in isolation isn't diagnostic — only the co-occurring literal special-token litter is. The extent is small: these are 2 of 300 instances (0.67%) in this one run.

Prometheus 2 / BiGGen-Bench (KAIST AI).text: The BiGGen-Bench-Results dataset published on Hugging Face (prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench-Results, data/llm_as_a_judge-0000{0,1}-of-00002.parquet) contains 68,805 rows across 99 models, with a `response` column holding full raw completion text alongside model_name, judge scores/feedback (prometheus_8x7b_*, gpt4_*, claude_score), and human_score. The schema contains no provider, generation-id, or finish_reason column anywhere. The 14 API-routed proprietary models — claude-3-haiku/sonnet/opus, gemini-1.0-pro/pro-1.5/flash-1.5, gpt-3.5-turbo-1106, gpt-4-0125-preview, gpt-4-1106-preview, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09, gpt-4o-2024-05-13, mistral-large/medium-hjpark, and qwen-110b-chat — each have exactly 695 rows (9,730 total), none empty. The three longest completions among these models are claude-3-opus-20240229/tool_usage_multi_step_8 at 8,811 characters, gpt-4-turbo-2024-04-09/grounding_json_csv_xml_0 at 8,063 characters, and gpt-4o-2024-05-13/theory_of_mind_writing_a_speech_6 at 7,510 characters, each ending in a complete, well-punctuated sentence rather than cut off mid-word. A substring search across all 9,730 rows for the chat-template artifacts `<|eot_id|>`, `<|im_start|>`, `</s>`, `[INST]`, and the U+FFFD replacement character returns zero hits. The project's GitHub README (prometheus-eval/prometheus-eval/BiGGen-Bench/README.md) states "With the help of litellm, you can use various APIs from different providers. We mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter" — a methods disclosure, not evidence of what actually happened — and run_api_inference.py sets max_tokens=2048 uniformly while delegating provider selection entirely to litellm with no explicit base_url or provider pin. This rules out word salad, truncation, and chat-template litter (F1, F3, F4) in the API-routed completions, but says nothing about which provider or quantization level actually served each request: no provider metadata exists in this schema to check (F2 stays open), and there is no matched full-precision arm to compare against (F15 is unaddressed).

openbench (Groq).text: openbench is infrastructure — a CLI/framework other people run against their own model+provider choice — not a project that itself produces or publishes a benchmark result set, transcripts, or a leaderboard. The repo root, the docs/ tree, the tests/ tree (including tests/integration), the README, and the announcement blog post were directly checked for any committed results/outputs/logs/transcripts directory, HF dataset, or leaderboard site; none exists. Per-run results are written to a local `./logs/` directory and optionally pushed to a user's own Hugging Face dataset via `--hub-repo`, but that is an opt-in per-user action, not a published artifact from openbench itself. Nothing is published by this project for a reader to inspect for F1-F11 style fingerprints — the OpenRouterAPI wrapper's behavior can only be assessed by code audit (already done) or by a third party who chooses to run openbench with OpenRouter and publish their own logs.
openbench (Groq).what_is_published: openbench is an evaluation harness/CLI tool (analogous to Inspect AI itself), not a paper or a study with its own benchmark run to publish. Its GitHub repo (github.com/groq/openbench) has no results/, outputs/, logs/, runs/, or transcripts/ directory committed — confirmed by listing the repo root and tests/ (only `__init__.py`, `conftest.py`, and unit/CLI test files like `test_groq_provider.py`, `test_vllm_provider.py`, `tests/integration/test_cli.py`; no fixture files containing recorded model completions). The README states evaluation results are written locally to `./logs/` at run time and are only pushed to a Hugging Face dataset if a user opts in via `--hub-repo` — that is a per-user runtime option, not something the openbench project itself has published. Groq's own announcement blog post ("OpenBench: Reproducible LLM Evals Made Easy," groq.com/blog/openbench-open-reproducible-evals) is purely promotional: it names example models (o3, Claude Opus 4, Qwen 3) hypothetically but contains no score tables, no transcripts, and no mention of OpenRouter. No hosted leaderboard for openbench itself was found (results.groq.com is a separate, unrelated Groq inference-speed benchmark page, not an openbench eval leaderboard).
openbench (Groq).evidence: github.com/groq/openbench root listing (via GitHub API): only .claude, .github, .vscode, docs, packages, scripts, src, tests + config/doc files — no results/outputs/logs/runs/transcripts dir. tests/ listing: unit test files only (test_groq_provider.py, test_vllm_provider.py, etc.) and tests/integration/ contains only __init__.py + test_cli.py (3065 bytes, no fixture data). README quote: "Check results in ./logs/ or view them in an interactive UI: `bench view`"; Hugging Face push is via optional `--hub-repo` flag. Blog post groq.com/blog/openbench-open-reproducible-evals: no score tables, no transcripts, no OpenRouter mention (confirmed via WebFetch summary). WebSearch for "openbench groq leaderboard" returned no openbench-specific leaderboard; results.groq.com is a separate inference-speed benchmark unrelated to openbench eval scores.

tau2-bench (Sierra AI).text: tau2-bench publishes raw, per-turn conversation trajectories for the one leaderboard submission that used the OpenRouter-routed "qwen_embeddings" retrieval config (gpt-5.2, banking_knowledge domain, 4 trials, hosted on S3 and linked from a committed submission.json) — real, inspectable data, not just aggregate numbers. On that file, the checks that apply come back clean: no finish_reason=="length" truncation, no special-token/chat-template litter, and no leaked API-error strings. However, the file (and the trajectory schema in general) never records a provider/generation-id for any API call — the one provider-shaped field present, provider_session_id, is null throughout — so mixed backends for the embedding calls specifically can neither be confirmed nor ruled out. Separately, OpenRouter here only ever returns embedding vectors, never decoded text, so the classic text-corruption fingerprints (token soup, chat-template litter, reasoning-shape drift) are structurally uninformative about this call path even when clean. The original arXiv paper's own committed results (data/tau2/results/final/*.json) predate the banking_knowledge domain entirely and never touch OpenRouter at all.
tau2-bench (Sierra AI).what_is_published: (1) The arXiv paper's own raw trajectories for its three original domains (airline/retail/telecom), committed at data/tau2/results/final/*.json — these never touch OpenRouter since banking_knowledge (the only OpenRouter-using domain) postdates the paper. (2) A public leaderboard (taubench.com) with 37+ submission.json files committed under web/leaderboard/public/submissions/, each carrying aggregate Pass^k numbers plus a `retrieval_config` field. (3) Full raw per-turn trajectory JSON files (tool calls, messages, timing, reward info) for most leaderboard submissions, hosted on S3 (bucket sierra-tau-bench-public) and linked from the committed submission.json manifests — genuinely downloadable, not just described. Only one such trajectory set uses the OpenRouter-backed "qwen_embeddings" retrieval config; it was downloaded and inspected directly (41.7MB, 388 simulations).
